steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.sunt: 'Sunt' is a finite verb but it is in the subordinate relative clause
hints.Caesaris: 'Caesaris' is a proper noun in the genitive case

hints.to read: 'To read' is 'legere' — 'Reddite' comes from 'reddere' (to give back)

steps.reveal: Reddite = Give back! Render! (2nd pl. imperative)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'redditis' — 'Reddite' lacks the -is ending
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd plural would be 'reddatis' — 'Reddite' is imperative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'reddidistis' — 'Reddite' is imperative

hints.redo, redire, redii, reditum: These are the parts of 'redire' (to go back) — 'reddere' means 'to give back'
hints.rego, regere, rexi, rectum: These are the principal parts of 'rego' (to rule/guide)
hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'do' (to give) — 'reddere' is the compound re- + dare but follows 3rd conjugation

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sint' — 'sunt' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edo' (to eat) — 'sunt' is from 'sum' (to be)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.possum, posse, potui, —: These are the parts of 'possum' (to be able) — a compound of 'sum'

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Caesar' — 'Caesaris' with -is ending is genitive

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'Caesaris' ends in -is

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Caesaris' — 'Caesari' with -i ending is dative

steps.reveal: quae sunt Caesaris = the things that are Caesar's

hints.interrogative pronoun: 'Quae' can be interrogative in a question, but here it introduces a relative clause
hints.indefinite adjective: An indefinite 'quae' follows 'si/ne' — here it is a relative pronoun
hints.exclamatory pronoun: An exclamatory 'quae!' expresses surprise — here it is a simple relative

steps.prompt: What type of genitive is 'Caesaris' in 'quae sunt Caesaris'?

hints.partitive genitive: Partitive genitive indicates 'part of' — here 'Caesaris' shows ownership
hints.subjective genitive: Subjective genitive shows the agent of an action — here it is simple possession
hints.genitive of description: Genitive of description qualifies a noun — 'Caesaris' shows who owns the things

translation: Render therefore to Caesar the things that are Caesar's
